preamble: The voiced palatal approximant /j/ can undergo fortition, a process where its degree of stricture increases, leading to its transformation into a voiced alveolo-palatal affricate /d͡ʑ/. This shift, known as affrication, involves the approximant becoming a stop that releases into a fricative.

examples.note: Latin initial /j/ (as in 'iocus' /jokus/ 'game') underwent fortition to a voiced alveolo-palatal affricate /d͡ʑ/ (as in Italian 'gioco' /d͡ʑɔko/) during the development of Romance languages.

In the Greek dialect of Astypalaia, the palatal approximant /j/ experienced a fortition process, passing through intermediate stages of a palatal fricative /ʝ/ and a palatal stop /ɟ/, ultimately resulting in a voiced alveolo-palatal affricate /d͡ʑ/. This shift is also attested in Late Vulgar Latin.

sources: The Phonetics and Phonology of Sibilants: A Synchronic and Diachronic OT Typology of Sibilant Inventories - Rutgers Optimality Archive
